english: Enter the world of German proverbs and oral tradition. Learn core literary vocabulary. Understand why proverbs matter so much in German culture.
Learn 18 words from the literary tradition
Understand proverbs as cultural wisdom
Distinguish Sprichwort, Redewendung, and Gleichnis
Join Heinrich on his Sunday visit

Sunday afternoon at Hildi's. The room smells of coffee and old wood. Heinrich sits in his armchair, a blanket on his lap. Hildi places cake on the table. Afternoon light filters through lace curtains. Verumius arrives with a notebook. He has learned to write things down.
Ah, Verumius! Sit down. Today I will tell you something important. No textbook. No grammar. Proverbs.
Proverbs? Like 'Practice makes perfect'?
Exactly! But a proverb is more than words. It is wisdom that has survived generations. Each one has a story.
He tells them slightly differently every time. But do not interrupt him.
proverb
Sprich (speak) + Wort (word). A complete sentence of folk wisdom.\nDas Sprichwort. Plural: die Sprichwörter.

A: Do you know a German proverb?
B: Yes! 'The early bird catches the worm.'
A: That is a nice proverb.
B: There are thousands of proverbs.
Literally a 'speak-word.' German has thousands of Sprichwörter, many dating back centuries. 'Morgenstund hat Gold im Mund' is one of the oldest. A Sprichwort is always a complete sentence of wisdom.
idiom, figure of speech
Rede (speech) + Wendung (turn). A fixed phrase used in sentences.\nDifferent from Sprichwort: not a complete sentence of wisdom.

A: What is a Redewendung?
B: A fixed expression like 'to put your foot in it.'
A: And a proverb?
B: A complete sentence of wisdom, like 'Practice makes perfect.'

Literally a 'speech-turn.' A Redewendung is a fixed phrase you use inside sentences, while a Sprichwort stands alone as a complete sentence. 'Ins Fettnäpfchen treten' is a Redewendung, not a Sprichwort.
wisdom
From weise (wise) + -heit (state of being). Die Weisheit.\nThe -heit suffix always creates feminine abstract nouns.

A: What do proverbs carry?
B: Old wisdom. Experience from centuries.
A: Is every piece of wisdom timeless?
B: Not all. Some wisdom no longer fits.

From 'weise' (wise) + '-heit' (state of being). 'Alte Weisheit' (ancient wisdom) is what Sprichwörter carry. 'Weisheitszahn' (wisdom tooth) is the bonus compound. The -heit suffix always makes feminine abstract nouns.
expression, manner of speaking
Reden (to speak) + Art (kind/manner). Broader than Redewendung.\nAny characteristic way of expressing something.

A: 'That is just a Redensart' means what?
B: It means: do not take it literally.
A: So a way of speaking?
B: Exactly. A particular way of expressing something.
'Reden' (to speak) + 'Art' (kind/manner). Broader than Redewendung. Any characteristic way of expressing something. 'Das ist so eine Redensart' dismisses literal interpretation.
fairy tale
From Maer (old word for story) + -chen (diminutive). Das Maerchen.\nThe diminutive suffix makes it neuter.

A: Do you know German fairy tales?
B: Yes, Little Red Riding Hood and Snow White.
A: Who collected the fairy tales?
B: The Brothers Grimm. In the 19th century.
The Brothers Grimm collected German Maerchen and made them world-famous. The diminutive '-chen' suffix gives it neuter gender and a gentle quality. From 'Maer,' an old word for story or news.
narrative, story
From erzählen (to narrate). More literary than Geschichte.\nA short literary work. Kurzerzählung = short story.

A: What is the difference between Erzählung and Geschichte?
B: Erzählung sounds more literary.
A: So a technical term?
B: Yes, for short literary works.
More literary than 'Geschichte' (story/history). A short literary work. 'Kurzerzählung' = short story. The formal word for narrative fiction. From 'erzählen' (to tell/narrate).
metaphor
From Greek 'metaphora' (transfer). Feminine in German.\n'Das Leben ist eine Reise' is a classic Metapher.

A: What is a metaphor?
B: An image for something else.
A: For example?
B: 'Life is a journey.' Life does not actually travel.

From Greek 'metaphora' (transfer). Feminine in German, though many learners guess neuter. 'Das Leben ist eine Reise' is a classic Metapher. German literary analysis loves identifying Metaphern in texts.
parable, allegory
From gleich (same/equal) + -nis (abstract noun suffix).\nBiblical parables are Gleichnisse.

A: What is a parable?
B: A simple story with deeper meaning.
A: Like in the Bible?
B: Yes, but Heinrich's stories are also parables.
From 'gleich' (same/equal) + '-nis' (abstract noun suffix). Biblical parables are 'Gleichnisse.' Heinrich's bakery stories are Gleichnisse: simple tales carrying deeper meaning.
poet, literary writer
From dichten (to compose poetry). Der Dichter.\nGoethe is 'der groesste deutsche Dichter.'

A: Who is the most famous German poet?
B: Goethe. Without doubt.
A: What did the poet write?
B: Poems, novels, plays. Everything.

'Dichter und Denker' (poets and thinkers) is how Germans describe their cultural tradition. The word covers poets and literary authors. Goethe is 'der groesste deutsche Dichter' (the greatest German poet).
poet, literary writer (female)
Dichter + -in. Die Dichterin.\nAnnette von Droste-Huelshoff, Ingeborg Bachmann.

A: Do you know a German female poet?
B: Ingeborg Bachmann. A magnificent poet.
A: What did the poet write?
B: Poems and narratives. Wonderful language.
Annette von Droste-Huelshoff, Else Lasker-Schüler, Ingeborg Bachmann: Germany's great Dichterinnen deserve the title. The -in suffix consistently creates feminine forms for professions.
literary
From Latin 'litterarius.' Literarische Werke = literary works.\n'Ein literarischer Abend' is a German cultural tradition.

A: Is that a literary text?
B: Yes, very literary. Poetic language.
A: What does 'literary' mean exactly?
B: Belonging to literature. Artistically written.
From Latin 'litterarius.' 'Ein literarischer Abend' (a literary evening) is a German cultural tradition: readings, wine, and discussion. 'Literarische Werke' (literary works) is the standard term.
poetic
From Greek 'poietikos' (creative). Poetische Sprache\nuses imagery, rhythm, and metaphor.

A: That sounds very poetic.
B: Thanks! Or do you mean that ironically?
A: No, really. Poetic language is beautiful.
B: When it is genuine, yes.
From Greek 'poietikos' (creative). 'Poetische Sprache' uses imagery, rhythm, and metaphor. 'Das klingt sehr poetisch' is both a compliment and sometimes gentle teasing.
timeless
Zeit (time) + -los (without). Timeless.\nThe -los suffix always means 'without': endlos, grenzenlos.

A: Are proverbs timeless?
B: The best ones, yes. They survive centuries.
A: Timeless wisdom then?
B: Exactly. It is valid today as it was 500 years ago.

'Zeit' (time) + '-los' (without). Timeless. 'Zeitlose Weisheit' is what good Sprichwörter carry. They outlive the people who coined them. The -los suffix always means 'without': zeitlos, endlos, grenzenlos.
folk, popular, traditional
Volk (people/folk) + tuemlch (characteristic of).\nCarries warmth and earthiness.

A: Are proverbs folk in nature?
B: Yes, they come from ordinary people, not scholars.
A: Folk language is simple?
B: Simple, but deep. That is the art.
'Volk' (people/folk) + 'tuemlch' (characteristic of). 'Volkstümlche Sprichwörter' come from ordinary people, not scholars. The word carries warmth and earthiness.
handed down, transmitted
Über (over) + liefert (delivered). Past participle of überliefern.\n'Mündlich überliefert' = orally transmitted.

A: How did proverbs survive?
B: They were handed down orally.
A: From generation to generation?
B: Yes. Transmitted wisdom needs no book.
'Über' (over) + 'liefert' (delivered). Past participle of 'überliefern.' 'Mündlich überliefert' (orally transmitted) describes how proverbs survived before writing. Delivered over the generations.
to tell, to narrate
Not separable. Different from sagen (to say):\nerzählen implies a story, sagen implies a statement.

A: Heinrich, tell us a story!
B: Gladly. I will tell you about my grandfather.
A: He narrates so vividly.
B: Yes, he is a born storyteller.
From old German 'irzellen' (to recount). Not separable. Different from 'sagen' (to say): erzählen implies a narrative with beginning, middle, and end. Sagen implies a single statement.
to compose poetry, to write literary works
From Latin 'dictare' (to dictate). Not separable.\nAlso colloquially: 'Das ist gedichtet!' (That is made up!).

A: Goethe composed poetry in Weimar.
B: What did he compose there?
A: Faust, poems, everything possible.
B: Composing poetry is an art.
From Latin 'dictare' (to dictate). 'Goethe dichtete in Weimar.' Also colloquially: 'Das ist gedichtet!' (That is made up!). Creating with words. The verb behind Dichter and Dichtung.
to quote
From Latin 'citare' (to summon). Not separable.\nEin Zitat (a quote) is the noun.

A: May I quote Heinrich?
B: Of course. What did he say?
A: I quote: 'The early bird catches the worm.'
B: That is a classic. Good quote.
From Latin 'citare' (to summon). 'Ich zitiere Heinrich' (I quote Heinrich). Not separable. 'Ein Zitat' (a quote) is the noun. Germans love zitieren in academic and professional contexts.
A complete sentence of folk wisdom, passed down for generations. Not an expression or idiom, but a full standalone saying.
The German word for 'wisdom'. What proverbs carry from generation to generation.
This old wisdom has survived generations.
From 'gleich' (same/equal). A simple story that stands for something deeper.
Learn the words of German literature. From poems and novels to epochs and genres. Master the vocabulary of the literary tradition.
Learn 20 words for literary genres and forms
Understand the three genres of literature
Describe poems, stories, and novels in German
Expand your literary vocabulary
Before I tell you my stories, you must know the language of stories. What is a poem? What is a narrative? Without the words, you cannot write the stories down.
poem
From dichten (to compose). Das Gedicht. Neuter because of Ge- prefix.\n'Ein Gedicht aufsagen' = to recite a poem.

A: Do you know a German poem?
B: Yes, 'The Erlking' by Goethe.
A: Can you recite the poem?
B: The beginning, yes. It is very well known.
Neuter because of the Ge- prefix. 'Ein Gedicht aufsagen' (to recite a poem) is a German school tradition. Schiller's 'Die Buergschaft' is the classic recitation piece.
verse, line of poetry
From Latin 'versus' (a turning, a line). Der Vers.\nEach line in a poem is a Vers.

A: How many verses does the poem have?
B: Twelve verses in three stanzas.
A: The first verse is the most beautiful.
B: Yes, it sets the tone for the whole poem.
From Latin 'versus' (a turning, a line). In German poetry, each line is a Vers. 'Freie Verse' (free verse) has no rhyme or fixed meter. 'Der erste Vers' sets the tone.
stanza
From Greek 'strophe' (turning). A group of Verse forming a unit.\nGerman poetry analysis always counts Strophen.

A: How many stanzas does the poem have?
B: Four stanzas with four verses each.
A: The last stanza is the strongest.
B: Yes, that is where the turning point comes.
From Greek 'strophe' (turning). A group of Verse forming a unit. 'Die erste Strophe' (the first stanza). German poetry analysis always counts Strophen and Verse.
rhyme
From Old French 'rime.' Der Reim.\n'Sich einen Reim auf etwas machen' = to make sense of something.

A: Do these words rhyme?
B: Yes, 'Haus' and 'Maus' are a rhyme.
A: Children learn through rhymes.
B: Yes, counting rhymes and children's songs.
From Old French 'rime.' 'Sich einen Reim auf etwas machen' (to make a rhyme out of something) means to make sense of something. German children learn Reime (nursery rhymes) early.
lyric poetry
From Greek 'lyrikos' (of the lyre). One of the three Gattungen.\nPersonal, emotional, rhythmic.

A: What is lyric poetry?
B: Poems. Personal, emotional texts.
A: And the difference from prose?
B: Lyric poetry has rhythm, rhymes, verses.
One of the three Gattungen (genres) of literature. Lyrik is personal, emotional, rhythmic. German Lyrik from Goethe to Celan is world-renowned. From Greek 'lyrikos' (of the lyre).
prose
From Latin 'prosa' (straightforward). Non-verse writing.\n'Prosaisch' means both 'in prose' and 'unimaginative.'

A: Is that prose or poetry?
B: Prose. No rhythm, no rhymes.
A: Prose sounds simpler.
B: Simpler, but not less artful.
From Latin 'prosa' (straightforward). The opposite of poetry. 'Prosaisch' as an adjective means both 'in prose' and 'unimaginative.' Context decides which meaning.
novel
From French 'roman.' Masculine. Der Bildungsroman\n(coming-of-age novel) is a German literary invention.

A: What novel are you reading right now?
B: A novel by Thomas Mann.
A: The novel is long, right?
B: Yes, but every sentence is worth it.
From French 'roman' (novel). 'Der Bildungsroman' (coming-of-age novel) is a German literary invention. Thomas Mann's novels are landmarks of German Roman tradition.
short story
Kurz (short) + Geschichte (story). Die Kurzgeschichte.\nPost-war German literature excelled at Kurzgeschichten.

A: What is a short story?
B: A short narrative with an open ending.
A: Who wrote good short stories?
B: Heinrich Boell and Wolfgang Borchert.
'Kurz' (short) + 'Geschichte' (story). Post-war German literature excelled at Kurzgeschichten. Heinrich Boell and Wolfgang Borchert wrote defining examples of the form.
novella
From Italian 'novella.' In German literary theory, a Novelle\nhas strict features: central conflict, turning point, symbolic 'Falke.'

A: Is a novella a short story?
B: Yes, but with strict rules.
A: What rules?
B: A turning point and a symbolic object, the 'falcon.'

From Italian 'novella' (short new story). In German literary theory, a Novelle has specific features: a central conflict, a turning point, and a 'Falke' (falcon, a symbolic object). Stricter than a Kurzgeschichte.
play, drama
From Greek 'drama' (action/deed). One of the three Gattungen.\n'Kein Drama!' = No big deal! (slang).

A: What is a drama?
B: A play. Dialogue on stage.
A: And in everyday life?
B: There you say 'No drama!' and mean: no problem.
One of the three Gattungen. Schiller and Brecht are Germany's great Dramatiker. 'Kein Drama!' (No drama!) is also everyday slang for 'no big deal.'
fable
From Latin 'fabula' (story). Animal stories with moral lessons.\nAlso means 'plot': die Fabel des Romans.

A: Do you know a fable?
B: Yes, the fox and the grapes.
A: What is the moral of the fable?
B: One despises what one cannot reach.
From Latin 'fabula' (story). Animal stories with moral lessons. German fables by Lessing are classics. The word also means 'plot': 'Die Fabel des Romans' (the plot of the novel). Double meaning.
genre, category
Three literary Gattungen: Epik (prose), Lyrik (poetry),\nDramatik (drama). Every text belongs to one of these.

A: Which genre does this text belong to?
B: Lyric poetry. It is a poem.
A: And a novel?
B: That belongs to epic. Narrative prose.
The three literary Gattungen: Epik (prose fiction), Lyrik (poetry), Dramatik (drama). Every text belongs to one. This system comes from ancient Greek poetics and German schools still teach it.
work (of art/literature)
From Germanic 'werk.' Das Werk von Kafka = the works of Kafka.\nAlso: factory (Automobilwerk).

A: Do you know Kafka's work?
B: Yes, especially 'The Metamorphosis.'
A: His work is timeless.
B: Yes, every work surprises anew.
From Germanic 'werk' (work/creation). 'Das Werk von Kafka' (the works of Kafka). Also: factory ('Automobilwerk'). A completed creation, the result of labor.
plot, action
From handeln (to act). 'Die Handlung spielt in Berlin.'\nAlso: a physical shop (Buchhandlung = bookshop).

A: Where does the plot take place?
B: In Berlin, in the 19th century.
A: Is the plot exciting?
B: Yes, full of twists and surprises.
From 'handeln' (to act). 'Die Handlung spielt in Berlin' (The plot takes place in Berlin). Also: a physical shop ('Buchhandlung' = bookshop). The thread of events in a story.
main character, protagonist
Haupt (main) + Figur (figure/character). Die Hauptfigur.\n'Wer ist die Hauptfigur?' is the first question in Literaturunterricht.

A: Who is the main character of the novel?
B: A young man named Werther.
A: Is the main character likeable?
B: Yes, but also tragic.
'Haupt' (main) + 'Figur' (figure/character). 'Wer ist die Hauptfigur?' (Who is the main character?) is the first question in German Literaturunterricht.
setting, scene
Schau (show/view) + Platz (place). Der Schauplatz.\nWhere the story unfolds. Literally a 'show-place.'

A: What is the setting of the story?
B: A small town in Saxony.
A: Why is the setting important?
B: It determines the atmosphere.
'Schau' (show/view) + 'Platz' (place). 'Der Schauplatz der Handlung' (the setting of the plot). Where the story unfolds. Literally a 'show-place.'
era, literary period
From Greek 'epoche' (stopping point). German literary history\nis organized by Epochen: Klassik, Romantik, Expressionismus.

A: From which era does this poem come?
B: From the Romantic period. Early 19th century.
A: What characterizes this era?
B: Emotion, nature, and longing.
From Greek 'epoche' (stopping point). German literary history is organized by Epochen: Klassik, Romantik, Expressionismus. Each Epoche has defining characteristics.
epic
In literary theory: belonging to Epik (narrative prose).\nIn slang: 'Das war episch!' (That was epic!).

A: Is 'The Lord of the Rings' epic?
B: Yes, in both meanings!
A: Literary and colloquial?
B: Exactly. Epic as a technical term and as slang.
Both formal literary term and modern youth slang. In literary theory: belonging to Epik (narrative prose). In slang: 'Das war episch!' (That was epic!). From Greek 'epikos.'
lyrical
From Greek 'lyrikos.' Belonging to Lyrik.\n'Lyrische Sprache' is emotionally charged and rhythmic.

A: This text has a lyrical quality.
B: Yes, almost like a poem.
A: Lyrical means poetic?
B: Similar, but lyrical refers to emotion and rhythm.
From Greek 'lyrikos.' 'Lyrische Sprache' (lyrical language) is emotionally charged and rhythmic. Also used loosely: 'ein lyrischer Moment' (a lyrical moment).
poetic, of a poet
From Dichter + -isch. 'Dichterische Freiheit' = poetic license.\nThe adjective form of being a Dichter.

A: He used poetic license.
B: What does that mean?
A: He bent the rules for art.
B: So artistic license?
From 'Dichter' + '-isch.' 'Dichterische Freiheit' (poetic license) means a writer can bend rules for art. 'Dichterisch begabt' (poetically gifted). The adjective form of being a Dichter.

German literary theory divides all literature into three genres:

1. Epik: narrative prose. Romane, Novellen, Kurzgeschichten, Maerchen, Fabeln. A narrator tells a story.

2. Lyrik: poetry. Gedichte, Verse, Strophen, Reime. Emotional, rhythmic, personal.

3. Dramatik: theatre. Dramen, Tragödien, Komoedien. Dialogue and stage directions, no narrator.

Every text belongs to one of these three genres. This system comes from ancient Greek poetics.

This classification system dates back to Aristotle's Poetics and was formalized in German literary theory by the Romantics.

Epik (Epic/Narrative): A narrator tells a story. Past tense is typical. Subtypes: Roman (novel), Novelle (novella), Kurzgeschichte (short story), Maerchen (fairy tale), Fabel (fable).

Lyrik (Lyric): Personal expression through rhythm and imagery. Present tense is common. Subtypes: Sonett, Ballade, Ode, Elegie, Haiku.

Dramatik (Dramatic): Action through dialogue. No narrator. Stage directions replace description. Subtypes: Tragödie, Komoedie, Tragikomoedie.

German schools test this classification regularly. Knowing the Gattung is the first step of any Textanalyse (text analysis).
German literary theory gives the Novelle strict rules: one central conflict, a turning point, and a 'Falke' (falcon symbol).
The word for 'plot' or 'action' of a story. From 'handeln' (to act).
The plot of the novel takes place in Berlin in the 19th century.
A 'Schau-Platz' (show-place). Where the events of the plot unfold.
Good. Now you know the words. Now I can tell you what my grandfather told me. And you can write it down. In the right words.
Learn Konjunktiv I for indirect speech. Master the vocabulary of literary analysis. Report what others say in formal German.
Learn 20 words for literary analysis and reporting
Understand Konjunktiv I as 'reporter mode'
Report what others say in formal German
Analyze literary style devices
Heinrich leans forward. His eyes brighten. He is about to tell a story he has told a hundred times, each time slightly different. Verumius's pen is ready.
My grandfather always said: 'The early bird catches the worm.' And he was right. He got up every morning at four o'clock. The bakery was his kingdom.
So: Heinrich told that his grandfather had started every morning at four o'clock. The bakery had been his kingdom.
It was half past three, not four. But keep telling, Papa.
quote, citation
From Latin 'citatum.' Ein bekanntes Zitat = a famous quote.\n'Zitat Ende' is said aloud in speeches.

A: Do you know a famous quote?
B: Yes: 'I know that I know nothing.'
A: Who is the quote from?
B: From Socrates. A timeless quote.
From Latin 'citatum' (something cited). 'Zitat Ende' (end quote) is said aloud in speeches. German essays always require Zitate with proper attribution.
context
From Latin 'contextus' (weaving together). Der Kontext.\n'Aus dem Kontext gerissen' = taken out of context.

A: What does the word mean in context?
B: Viewed in context, the meaning changes.
A: Without context, you understand nothing.
B: Exactly. Context is everything.
From Latin 'contextus' (weaving together). 'Im Kontext betrachtet' (viewed in context) changes meaning. 'Aus dem Kontext gerissen' = taken out of context.
perspective, point of view
From Latin 'perspectivus.' Die Perspektive.\nLiterary analysis always asks: whose Perspektive is this?

A: From whose perspective is the story?
B: From the narrator's perspective.
A: And Heinrich's perspective?
B: That is naturally subjective.
From Latin 'perspectivus' (of looking through). 'Aus Heinrichs Perspektive' (from Heinrich's perspective). Literary analysis always asks: whose Perspektive is this?
motif, theme
From Latin 'motivum' (moving). A recurring element in a text.\nAlso means 'motive' for an action.

A: What is the central motif?
B: Bread. It keeps appearing.
A: So a motif is a recurring element?
B: Yes, it runs through the story.
From Latin 'motivum' (moving). A recurring element in a text. 'Das Brot-Motiv' (the bread motif) runs through Heinrich's stories. Also means 'motive' for an action.
symbol
From Greek 'symbolon' (token/sign). Das Symbol.\nSomething concrete that represents something abstract.

A: What does bread symbolize in Heinrich's stories?
B: Community and work.
A: A symbol stands for something else?
B: Yes, something concrete for something abstract.
From Greek 'symbolon' (token/sign). 'Brot ist ein Symbol für Gemeinschaft' (Bread is a symbol of community). Something concrete that represents something abstract.
comparison, simile
From vergleichen (to compare). A simile uses 'wie' (like).\nDifferent from Metapher: Vergleich states likeness, not identity.

A: What is a comparison?
B: 'Life is like a book.' With 'like.'
A: And a metaphor?
B: 'Life is a book.' Without 'like.'
A simile uses 'wie' (like) or 'als' (as). Different from a Metapher, which states identity directly. 'Ein Vergleich: Das Leben ist wie ein Buch.'
pun, play on words
Wort (word) + Spiel (game/play). Das Wortspiel.\nGermans love Wortspiele despite their reputation for no humor.

A: Was that a pun?
B: Yes! Did you notice it?
A: A successful pun.
B: Thanks. Puns are an art.
'Wort' (word) + 'Spiel' (game/play). Germans love Wortspiele, though they are famously accused of having no humor. 'Ein gelungenes Wortspiel' (a successful pun) earns groans and respect.
ambiguity, double meaning
Doppel (double) + Deutigkeit (meaning-ness).\nMany Sprichwörter work through Doppeldeutigkeit.

A: This word has a double meaning.
B: It can mean two things?
A: Yes, that makes it interesting.
B: Ambiguities are typical for proverbs.
'Doppel' (double) + 'Deutigkeit' (meaning-ness). Having two possible interpretations. Many Sprichwörter work through Doppeldeutigkeit.
allusion, reference
From anspielen auf (to allude to). An indirect reference.\nSubtlety as art. Rewards those who recognize it.

A: Was that an allusion to Goethe?
B: Yes, a subtle allusion.
A: Not everyone understands allusions.
B: That is the charm. You have to recognize it.
From 'anspielen auf' (to allude to). 'Eine Anspielung auf Goethe' (an allusion to Goethe). Indirect reference that rewards those who recognize it. Subtlety as art.
reading material, text being read
From Latin 'lectura' (reading). Die Lektüre.\n'Pflichtlektüre' = required reading.

A: What is your current reading?
B: Kafka. Difficult but fascinating reading.
A: Is that required reading?
B: No, voluntary. The best reading is voluntary.
From Latin 'lectura' (reading). 'Pflichtlektüre' (required reading) is what German students must read. The word means both the text and the act of reading it.
allegedly, supposedly
An + geblich (from geben). Signals doubt about what is reported.\nA distancing adverb for unverified claims.

A: Allegedly he started at four o'clock.
B: Allegedly? You do not believe it?
A: Hildi says it was half past three.
B: So 'four o'clock' is only allegedly correct.
Signals doubt about what is being reported. 'Angeblich hat er um vier Uhr angefangen' casts doubt on the claim. A distancing adverb.
apparently
An + scheinend (appearing). Based on appearances.\nSlightly less certain than offenbar, more than angeblich.

A: Apparently it was half past three.
B: That seems likely.
A: Apparently is not certain?
B: No, it is based on what one sees.
'An' + 'scheinend' (appearing). Based on appearances. Slightly less certain than 'offenbar' but more confident than 'angeblich.' The scale: angeblich < anscheinend < offenbar.
as is well known
Bekannt (known) + -lich. Signals shared knowledge.\nAcademic and journalistic register.

A: As is well known, proverbs are timeless.
B: Yes, everyone knows that.
A: 'Bekanntlich' sounds very formal.
B: It fits in essays and speeches.
'Bekannt' (known) + '-lich.' Signals shared knowledge. The speaker assumes everyone knows this. Academic and journalistic register.
obviously, evidently
Offen (open) + -bar (visible). Something openly visible.\nStronger than anscheinend, weaker than sicher.

A: Obviously Heinrich was right.
B: Yes, that is fairly clear.
A: Offenbar is stronger than anscheinend?
B: Yes, offenbar means: it is almost certain.
'Offen' (open) + 'bar' (visible/bearable). Something openly visible. Stronger than 'anscheinend,' weaker than 'sicher.'
according to (preposition)
Originally 'loud,' but as preposition: 'according to.'\nLaut + dative/genitive. Compact attribution.

A: According to Heinrich, the day began at four.
B: According to Hildi, it was half past three.
A: According to whom should I believe?
B: According to the truth. It lies in between.
Originally 'loud,' but as a preposition: 'according to.' 'Laut Heinrich begann der Tag um vier.' A compact way to attribute a statement. Preposition + dative or genitive.
to claim, to assert
Be- + haupten (from Haupt = head). Not separable.\nImplies the statement may not be verified.

A: Heinrich claims he never overslept.
B: That is what he claims.
A: Behaupten sounds doubtful?
B: Yes, you do not know if it is true.
'Be-' + 'haupten' (from 'Haupt' = head). To claim, to assert. Implies the statement may not be verified. Not separable.
to report
Be- + richten (to direct/set right). Factual reporting.\nMore neutral than behaupten. Not separable.

A: Verumius reports what Heinrich tells.
B: Berichten is neutral?
A: Yes, without judgment.
B: Different from behaupten, which implies doubt.
'Be-' + 'richten' (to direct/set right). Factual reporting. More neutral than 'behaupten.' Not separable. 'Bericht' (report) is the noun.
to emphasize
Be- + Ton (tone/emphasis). Placing weight on something.\nNot separable. 'Betonung' is the noun.

A: Heinrich emphasized that bread was important.
B: He placed special value on that.
A: Betonen means to highlight?
B: Yes, to give something special emphasis.
'Be-' + 'Ton' (tone/emphasis). Placing tone/weight on something. Not separable. 'Betonung' (emphasis, stress) is the noun.
to describe, to depict vividly
From Schild (sign/shield). More vivid than beschreiben.\nPainting a picture with words.

A: Heinrich describes the bakery in all details.
B: You can almost smell it.
A: Schildern is more vivid than beschreiben?
B: Yes, schildern paints a picture with words.
From 'Schild' (sign/shield). More vivid than 'beschreiben.' Painting a picture with words. Originally: to paint a sign. 'Er schildert die Backstube in allen Details.'
to mean, to think, to be of the opinion
From old German 'meinen' (to think/intend).\n'Was meinen Sie?' = What do you think?

A: What do you think about that?
B: I think Heinrich is right.
A: He was of the opinion that the old times were better.
B: Many older people think that.
'Was meinen Sie?' (What do you think?) is the polite way to ask for an opinion. 'Er meinte, die alten Zeiten seien besser gewesen' uses Konjunktiv I for indirect speech.

German has a special verb form for reported speech: the Konjunktiv I.

Formation: verb stem + endings (-e, -est, -e, -en, -et, -en).

sein: er sei
haben: er habe
können: er könne
sagen: er sage

Example: 'Heinrich sagte, die Backstube sei sein Königreich.'

The Konjunktiv I signals: 'I am reporting, not giving my own opinion.'

Usage: newspapers, academic writing, formal language.
In everyday speech, many speakers use Konjunktiv II (wäre, hätte) instead of Konjunktiv I, but B2 learners must be able to recognize and produce the Konjunktiv I.

The Konjunktiv I is formed from the verb stem (infinitive minus -en) plus endings: -e, -est, -e, -en, -et, -en.

When Konjunktiv I is identical to the indicative (e.g., 'sie haben' = 'sie haben'), German switches to Konjunktiv II instead: 'sie hätten.' This happens mainly in ich and wir/sie forms.

The Konjunktiv I is used in:
1. Newspapers and news broadcasts
2. Academic writing
3. Formal speech and reports
4. Legal documents

In everyday speech, most Germans use Konjunktiv II or 'würde + Infinitiv' for indirect speech. But recognizing and producing Konjunktiv I is essential at B2 level.
This special verb form signals that you are reporting someone else's words, not your own opinion.
In reporter mode the auxiliary verb changes. Past tense + Partizip II gives the reported past.
Heinrich told that he had gotten up at four o'clock every morning.
Master 'als ob' + Konjunktiv II for unreal comparisons. Learn literary character and genre vocabulary. Combine indirect speech with hypothetical comparison.
Master 'als ob' + Konjunktiv II for unreal comparisons
Learn vocabulary for characters and literary styles
Understand the short form: 'als wäre' (inversion)
Combine Konjunktiv I (speech) with Konjunktiv II (comparison)

Heinrich continues telling. He describes the bakery as if he could still smell the flour dust. His hands move as if kneading invisible dough. Hildi shakes her head but smiles. Verumius writes and writes.
He always acts as if he knew everything. As if he had been the only baker in the world. When in fact Grandma ran half the business.
narrator
From erzählen (to narrate). Der Erzaehler.\nIn Literaturanalyse: 'der allwissende Erzaehler' = omniscient narrator.

A: Who is the narrator of this story?
B: An omniscient narrator. He knows everything.
A: And Heinrich? Is he also a narrator?
B: Yes, a born narrator.
In literary analysis: 'der allwissende Erzaehler' (the omniscient narrator) knows everything. 'Der Ich-Erzaehler' narrates in first person. Heinrich is a natural Erzaehler.
narrator (female)
Erzaehler + -in. Die Erzaehlerin.\n'Die unzuverlässiige Erzaehlerin' = the unreliable narrator.

A: Is the narrator reliable?
B: No, she is an unreliable narrator.
A: You cannot trust her?
B: Exactly. She changes the facts.
'Die unzuverlässiige Erzaehlerin' (the unreliable narrator) is a literary device. The reader cannot fully trust what she reports. The -in suffix creates the feminine form.
secondary character
Neben (beside/secondary) + Figur (figure/character).\nThe supporting cast. Essential but not the star.

A: Is Hildi a secondary character?
B: In Heinrich's stories, yes.
A: Secondary characters enrich the plot.
B: Without them the story would be flat.
'Neben' (beside/secondary) + 'Figur' (figure/character). 'Nebenfiguren bereichern die Handlung' (Secondary characters enrich the plot). Not the star, but essential.
tragedy
From Greek 'tragodia' (goat song). Die Tragödie.\nSchiller's 'Die Raeuber' and Goethe's 'Faust' are world literature.

A: Is 'Faust' a tragedy?
B: Yes, one of the greatest German tragedies.
A: What defines a tragedy?
B: The hero fails because of his fate.
From Greek 'tragodia' (goat song). One of the oldest dramatic forms. German Tragödien by Schiller and Goethe are world literature. 'Das ist eine Tragödie!' is also everyday drama.
poetry, poetic quality
From Greek 'poiesis' (making/creating). Broader than Lyrik:\nPoesie is the beauty and art in language itself.

A: Is there poetry in proverbs?
B: Yes, everyday poetry. Simple and beautiful.
A: Poetry is more than poems?
B: Yes, poetry is beauty in language.
From Greek 'poiesis' (making/creating). Broader than Lyrik: Poesie is the beauty and art in language itself. 'Alltägliche Poesie' (everyday poetry) is found in Sprichwörter.
writer, author
Schrift (writing) + Steller (one who places). Der Schriftsteller.\nMore prestige than 'Autor.'

A: Is Thomas Mann an author or a Schriftsteller?
B: A Schriftsteller. That sounds more literary.
A: What is the difference?
B: Schriftsteller write literary works.
'Schrift' (writing) + 'Steller' (one who places). More prestige than 'Autor': a Schriftsteller creates literary works. 'Freier Schriftsteller' (freelance writer) is a German tax category.
writer, author (female)
Schriftsteller + -in. Die Schriftstellerin.\nHerta Mueller (Nobel Prize), Juli Zeh: great Schriftstellerinnen.

A: Do you know a German female writer?
B: Herta Mueller. She won the Nobel Prize.
A: An important writer.
B: Yes, her works are powerful.
Christa Wolf, Herta Mueller (Nobel Prize), Juli Zeh: Germany's great Schriftstellerinnen shape contemporary German literature. The -in suffix creates the feminine form.
contemporary
Zeit (time) + Genosse (companion). Der Zeitgenosse.\nSomeone who lives in the same era.

A: Was Schiller a contemporary of Goethe?
B: Yes, they lived in the same era.
A: And colloquially?
B: 'A strange Zeitgenosse' means: a weird fellow.
'Zeit' (time) + 'Genosse' (companion). 'Goethes Zeitgenossen' (Goethe's contemporaries). Also colloquially: 'ein seltsamer Zeitgenosse' (a strange fellow).
fictional
From Latin 'fictivus' (feigned). The opposite of real/wahr.\n'Fiktive Figuren' do not exist but feel real.

A: Is Verumius fictional?
B: Yes, a fictional character.
A: But he feels real.
B: That is good fiction.
From Latin 'fictivus' (feigned). 'Eine fiktive Geschichte' (a fictional story). The opposite of 'real' or 'wahr.' Fictional characters do not exist but feel real in good literature.
classic, classical
From Latin 'classicus.' In German: 'die Klassik' is a specific\nEpoche (Goethe and Schiller era, ~1786-1832).

A: Is that classical literature?
B: Yes, from the Weimar Classical period.
A: Klassisch also means 'typical'?
B: Yes. 'That is classic!' means: typical!
'Die Klassik' is a specific Epoche in German literary history (Goethe and Schiller era, ~1786-1832). Also everyday: 'Das ist klassisch!' (That is classic/typical!).
tragic
From Greek 'tragikos.' Related to Tragödie.\n'Tragischerweise' (tragically) is the adverb.

A: Does the story have a tragic ending?
B: Yes, unfortunately. The main character fails.
A: Tragic stories are often the best.
B: Because they are honest.
From Greek 'tragikos.' Related to Tragödie. 'Tragischerweise' (tragically) is the adverb. Heinrich's stories sometimes have tragische Elemente.
satirical
From Latin 'satiricus.' Using humor to critique society.\nGerman Satire tradition: from Heinrich Heine to Jan Boehmermann.

A: Is this text satirical?
B: Yes, it criticizes society with humor.
A: German satire is often sharp.
B: Sharp, but on point.
German Satire has a long tradition: from Heinrich Heine to Jan Boehmermann. 'Ein satirischer Kommentar' uses humor to critique society.
ironic
From Greek 'eironeia' (dissimulation). Saying the opposite\nof what you mean. German Ironie is just more subtle.

A: That was meant ironically.
B: I did not recognize it.
A: German irony is subtle.
B: Yes, you have to hear the tone of voice.
From Greek 'eironeia' (dissimulation). Saying the opposite of what you mean. Germans are accused of not understanding irony, but German Ironie is just more subtle.
humorous
Humor + voll (full of). German humor exists.\nIt is just structured differently.

A: Heinrich's stories are humorous.
B: Yes, in a dry, warm way.
A: German humor is dry?
B: Dry, but deep. And surprising.
'Humor' + 'voll' (full of). German humor exists. It is just structured differently. Heinrich's stories are humorvoll in a dry, warm way.
metaphorical
From Metapher + -isch. 'Metaphorisch gesprochen'\nintroduces a figurative comparison.

A: Bread as a metaphorical symbol?
B: Yes, metaphorically bread stands for community.
A: Metaphorically speaking then.
B: Exactly. Not literally, but figuratively.
From 'Metapher' + '-isch.' 'Metaphorisch gesprochen' (metaphorically speaking) introduces a figurative comparison. 'Brot als metaphorisches Symbol' in Heinrich's tales.
symbolic, allegorical
Sinn (sense/meaning) + Bild (image) + -lich.\nA German alternative to 'symbolisch.' Image carries meaning.

A: The bread is symbolic of work.
B: Symbolic? So symbolically?
A: Yes, the image (bread) carries the meaning (work).
B: A beautiful word.
'Sinn' (sense/meaning) + 'Bild' (image) + '-lich.' 'Sinnbildlich für das Leben' (symbolic of life). A German alternative to 'symbolisch.' The image (Bild) carries meaning (Sinn).
as if
Als (as) + ob (if/whether). Introduces unreal comparison.\nAlways followed by Konjunktiv II. Verb goes to END.

A: He acts as if he knew everything.
B: Knew? Why not 'knows'?
A: Because it is not real. 'Als ob' requires Konjunktiv II.
B: So: he does not really know?
'Als' (as) + 'ob' (if/whether). 'Er tut als ob er alles wüsste' (He acts as if he knew everything). Always followed by Konjunktiv II. Verb goes to the END of the clause.
as if (colloquial)
Als + wenn. Same meaning as als ob. Slightly more colloquial.\nSame grammar: Konjunktiv II, verb at end.

A: He speaks as if he were a poet.
B: Is that the same as 'als ob'?
A: Yes, just slightly more colloquial.
B: So als ob = als wenn?
'Als' + 'wenn.' Same meaning as 'als ob': as if. Slightly more colloquial. Same grammar: Konjunktiv II, verb at end.
as if (informal)
Wie (how/like) + wenn (if/when). The most informal variant.\n'Es fühlt sich an, wie wenn man nach Hause kommt.'

A: It feels like when you come home.
B: Wie wenn? That sounds informal.
A: Yes, people say that in everyday speech.
B: So the casual version of 'als ob'?
'Wie' (how/like) + 'wenn' (if/when). The most informal of the three. Same structure as als ob/als wenn but with 'wie' instead of 'als.'
as if (were) (literary short form)
Als + wäre (Konjunktiv II of sein). The SHORT form:\nals alone, verb directly after (inversion). No ob/wenn needed.

A: He speaks as if he were an expert.
B: No 'ob' after 'als'?
A: No, that is the short form. More elegant.
B: So: als + verb immediately after?
The SHORT form: 'als' alone, with verb directly after (inversion). No 'ob' or 'wenn' needed. More elegant and literary. 'Er spricht, als wäre er ein Experte.'

German compares reality with imagination using 'als ob.' The Konjunktiv II signals that something is NOT reality, it only LOOKS that way.

Two main patterns exist:
- Als ob with the verb at the END of the clause: 'Er tut, als ob er alles wüsste.'
- Als alone with INVERSION (no ob needed): 'Er tut, als wüsste er alles.' This short form is more elegant and literary.

Both mean the same thing. Two informal variants also exist: 'als wenn' (slightly colloquial) and 'wie wenn' (most informal). All four patterns take Konjunktiv II.

Common verb forms after als ob: wäre, hätte, wüsste, könnte.

In Heinrich's stories: 'Er erzählte es, als wäre er noch jung.' He is no longer young, but it LOOKS that way.

The four patterns:
1. als ob + Konj. II + verb END: Er tut, als ob er reich wäre.
2. als + Konj. II + INVERSION: Er tut, als wäre er reich.
3. als wenn + Konj. II + verb END: Er tut, als wenn er reich wäre.
4. wie wenn + Konj. II + verb END: Er tut, wie wenn er reich wäre.

All four mean the same thing. Patterns 1-2 are standard. Pattern 2 is most elegant. Pattern 3 is common in speech. Pattern 4 is informal.

Important: The 'würde + Infinitiv' form also works after als ob: 'Er tut, als ob er alles wissen würde.' Grammatically correct but less elegant than Konjunktiv II directly.

Recognition tip: whenever you see 'als ob,' 'als wenn,' 'wie wenn,' or 'als' followed immediately by a Konjunktiv II verb, you are looking at an unreal comparison.
After 'als' with this verb form, you compare reality with imagination. He speaks AS IF, but reality is different.
After 'als ob' the verb must be in Konjunktiv II. Recall the Konjunktiv II form of the verb 'sein' from the paradigm.
He talks about bread as if it were the most important thing in the world.
Two different verb forms: one reports Heinrich's words, the other describes a state that is not real.
to exist
From Latin 'existere' (to stand out, to come into being).\nRegular -ieren verb. 'Noch existieren' = to still exist.

A: Does the old bakery still exist?
B: No, it no longer exists.
A: When did it stop existing?
B: Twenty years ago. But in Heinrich's stories it goes on existing.

From Latin 'existere' (to stand out, to come into being). Borrowed into German via French. 'Die Existenz' (existence) is the noun. 'Das existenzielle Problem' (the existential problem). German Existenzphilosophie (existentialism) made this root famous worldwide.
The 'als ob' construction: als ob + Konjunktiv II at the end. The bakery no longer exists.
Heinrich tells stories as if he were still thirty. As if time had no power over the bakery. I write down what he says. But I also write down how he says it. That is almost more important.
Heinrich remembers the bakery of the 1960s. Learn how to shift tenses in indirect speech, distinguish literal from figurative meaning, and report narratives correctly.
Master tense shifting in indirect speech
Distinguish literal and figurative meaning
Report narratives precisely in formal German
The scene shifts. Golden-sepia light. A 1960s bakery. Wooden tables covered in flour. Ovens glowing. Young Heinrich kneads dough at 4 AM. The smell of fresh bread fills the memory.
My grandfather always said: 'The apple does not fall far from the tree.' He meant I was just like him. And he was right. I took over the bakery.
So: Heinrich reported that his grandfather had said the apple does not fall far from the tree. He had meant that Heinrich was like him.
Papa likes to exaggerate. But in the figurative sense it is true.
the practice, the exercise
Feminine noun. From 'üben' (to practice).\n'Übung macht den Meister' (practice makes the master).

A: How did you learn German so well?
B: Practice! Practice makes the master.
A: That is true. How much practice?
B: At least one hour every day.
'Übung macht den Meister' is one of Germany's most beloved Sprichwörter. Every German knows it by heart. The word comes from 'üben' (to practice), and the -ung suffix creates a feminine action noun.
the beginning
Masculine noun. From 'anfangen' (to begin).\n'Aller Anfang ist schwer' (every beginning is hard).

A: The beginning was really hard.
B: Yes, every beginning is hard.
A: But you did not give up.
B: No, after the beginning it gets easier.

'An' + 'Fang' (catching). A beginning catches the first moment. 'Am Anfang' (at the beginning), 'von Anfang an' (from the very start). The proverb 'Aller Anfang ist schwer' captures a universal truth.
figurative, metaphorical
'Bild' (image) + '-lich.' The opposite of 'wörtlich.'\n'Bildlich gesprochen' (figuratively speaking).

A: Do you mean that figuratively or literally?
B: Figuratively! Language is a bridge.
A: That is a beautiful image.
B: Proverbs are almost always figurative.
From 'Bild' (image/picture). Using images to convey meaning. Sprichwörter are often bildlich: 'Morgenstund hat Gold im Mund' does not mean there is actual gold in anyone's mouth.
literal, word-for-word
'Wort' (word) + '-lich.' The opposite of 'bildlich.'\n'Wörtlich genommen' (taken literally).

A: That is not meant literally.
B: I know. Literally it would be nonsensical.
A: Exactly. Proverbs are rarely literal.
B: You have to understand the figurative meaning.
From 'Wort' (word). 'Wörtlich genommen' (taken literally) means understanding words at face value. 'Das ist nicht wörtlich gemeint' warns against surface interpretation.
quiet, still
Also an adverb. 'Stille Wasser sind tief' (still waters run deep).\n'Die Stille' (the silence) is the noun.

A: Why are you so quiet?
B: Still waters run deep, they say.
A: So you are thinking?
B: Yes. Being quiet does not mean being empty.
This Sprichwort warns that quiet people may have deep thoughts or hidden depths. Related to English 'still.' 'Die Stille' (silence) is the noun. Do not underestimate the silent ones.
in the figurative sense
Fixed phrase. 'Im' (in the) + 'übertragenen' (transferred) + 'Sinne' (sense).\nIntroduces metaphorical interpretation.

A: Bread is the most important thing, said Heinrich.
B: In the figurative sense that means: fundamentals count.
A: And in the literal sense?
B: There it simply means: bread is delicious.
The idea has been 'transferred' (übertragen) from literal to symbolic. 'Im übertragenen Sinne bedeutet das...' is the standard phrase for introducing a metaphorical interpretation in German.
proverbial
'Sprichwort' + '-lich.' Something so well-known it has become a saying.\n'Die sprichwörtliche Puenktlichkeit.'

A: Germans are proverbially punctual.
B: Yes, the proverbial German punctuality!
A: Is that really true?
B: Mostly. It has become proverbial.

When something is so well-known it has become like a proverb. 'Die sprichwörtliche deutsche Puenktlichkeit' (the proverbial German punctuality). Also: 'sprichwörtlich ins Fettnäpfchen treten' (proverbially putting your foot in it).
ambiguous, double-meaning
'Zwei' (two) + 'deutig' (meaning). Having two interpretations.\n'Ein zweideutiger Witz' (an ambiguous joke).

A: This proverb is ambiguous.
B: Yes, it has two meanings.
A: Which one do you mean?
B: Both! That is the beauty of ambiguous expressions.
From 'zwei' (two) + 'deutig' (meaning). Many Sprichwörter are deliberately zweideutig, carrying a surface meaning and a deeper one. 'Eindeutig' (unambiguous) is the opposite.
colloquial, informal
'Umgang' (interaction) + 'sprachlich' (linguistic).\nThe register of everyday speech.

A: Is 'cool' colloquial?
B: Yes, that is colloquial.
A: And 'ausgezeichnet'?
B: That is more formal. Not colloquial.
The register of everyday speech. 'Umgangssprachlich sagt man...' (colloquially, one says...) introduces informal alternatives. Many Redewendungen are umgangssprachlich. Academic writing avoids them.
parable-like, allegorical
'Gleichnis' (parable) + '-haft' (having the quality of).\nDescribes stories with deeper meaning.

A: Heinrich's stories are parable-like.
B: Yes, they always have a deeper meaning.
A: Like a parable from the Bible?
B: Similar. Simple on the surface, deep beneath.
Heinrich's bakery stories are gleichnishaft: simple tales about bread and flour that carry wisdom about life. The '-haft' suffix means 'having the quality of,' like English '-ish' or '-like.'
prosaic, unimaginative
From 'Prosa' (prose) + '-isch.'\nDouble meaning: belonging to prose (neutral) or being dull (negative).

A: That sounds rather prosaic.
B: Yes, it lacks poetry.
A: Can you say it more poetically?
B: Instead of 'It is raining' perhaps 'The sky weeps.'
A word with two faces. In literary theory, 'prosaisch' simply means 'in prose' (neutral). In everyday speech, it means 'unimaginative' or 'dull' (negative). Context decides which meaning applies.
linguistic, language-related
'Sprache' (language) + '-lich.' Used in literary analysis.\n'Sprachliche Mittel' (linguistic devices).

A: What linguistic devices does Heinrich use?
B: Metaphors, comparisons, and proverbs.
A: He is linguistically very gifted.
B: Yes, a natural storyteller.
'Sprachliche Mittel' (linguistic devices) is the standard term in German literary analysis. 'Sprachlich begabt' (linguistically talented) is high praise. From 'Sprache' (language) + '-lich.'
written, in writing
'Schrift' (writing) + '-lich.' The opposite of 'mündlich.'\n'Bitte bestätigen Sie schriftlich' (please confirm in writing).

A: Is the exam written or oral?
B: Written. You have to write an essay.
A: And the written tradition?
B: That only began when someone wrote proverbs down.

'Schriftliche Überlieferung' (written tradition) is how stories survive on paper. The opposite of 'mündlich.' 'Bitte bestätigen Sie schriftlich' (please confirm in writing) is standard German bureaucracy.
oral, verbal
'Mund' (mouth) + '-lich.' The opposite of 'schriftlich.'\n'Mündliche Überlieferung' (oral tradition).

A: Proverbs were passed down orally.
B: Yes, from generation to generation.
A: Without books?
B: Exactly. Only through oral transmission.
'Mündliche Überlieferung' (oral tradition) is how Sprichwörter survived for centuries before anyone wrote them down. 'Mündliche Prüfung' (oral exam). Through the mouth, not on paper.
figurative, transferred
Past participle of 'übertragen' (to transfer).\n'Im übertragenen Sinn' (in the figurative sense).

A: What is the figurative meaning?
B: The meaning beyond the words.
A: So not literal?
B: Exactly. Transferred means: from literal to figurative.
When meaning is 'carried over' from literal to symbolic. 'Übertragene Bedeutung' = figurative meaning. The prefix 'über' (over) + 'tragen' (to carry) creates a word about meaning crossing boundaries.
to express, to utter
From 'äußer' (outer). Making inner thoughts outer.\nReflexive: 'sich äußern zu' (to comment on). Not separable.

A: Would you like to express your opinion?
B: Yes, I would like to comment on that.
A: Then express yourself freely.
B: Heinrich often uttered wisdom over coffee.
Making inner thoughts outer. From 'äußer' (outer). Reflexive: 'sich äußern zu' (to comment on). 'Er äußerte seine Meinung' (He expressed his opinion). Not separable.
to concede, to admit
'Ein' (in) + 'raeumen' (from 'Rahmen,' frame). Separable.\nMaking room for the other side's point.

A: Do you concede that it was hard?
B: Yes, I concede that the beginning was tough.
A: But you persevered.
B: I gladly concede that.
'Ein' (in) + 'raeumen' (to make room). Making room for the other side's point. Separable: 'Er raeumte ein, dass die Zeiten hart waren.' More formal than 'zugeben' (to admit).
to add (to what was said)
'Hinzu' (in addition) + 'fuegen' (to join). Separable.\n'Er fuegte hinzu, dass...' (He added that...).

A: Would you like to add something?
B: Yes, I would like to add that Heinrich was right.
A: What else did he add?
B: He added that bread meant everything.
Adding something to what has already been said. Separable: 'Er fuegte hinzu, dass...' The 'hinzu' prefix means 'in addition to.' 'Hinzufuegung' is the noun form.
to inform, to communicate
'Mit' (with) + 'teilen' (to share). Separable.\n'Er teilte mir mit, dass...' (He informed me that...).

A: I would like to inform you of something.
B: What do you want to tell me?
A: Heinrich informed me that he is coming back next Sunday.
B: That is nice news!
Sharing information with someone. 'Mit' (with) + 'teilen' (to share/divide). Separable: 'Er teilte mir mit, dass...' 'Mitteilung' (notification, message) is the noun. More formal than simply 'sagen.'
to remark, to note
'An' (at) + 'merken' (to notice). Separable.\nLighter than 'betonen' (to emphasize).

A: May I remark on something?
B: Of course, go ahead.
A: Heinrich remarked that getting up early was important.
B: A good remark.

Making a note of something in conversation. 'An' (at) + 'merken' (to notice/mark). Separable: 'Er merkte an, dass...' Lighter than 'betonen' (to emphasize). 'Anmerkung' (remark, annotation) is the noun.

Reported speech in Konjunktiv I follows three tense-shift rules.

- Present tense in direct speech shifts to Konjunktiv I present: 'Die Backstube IST wunderbar' becomes 'Er sagte, die Backstube SEI wunderbar.'
- Past tense shifts to Konjunktiv I past, using habe or sei plus Partizip II: 'Ich HABE um vier angefangen' becomes 'Er sagte, er HABE um vier angefangen.'
- Future tense shifts to Konjunktiv I future, using werde plus Infinitiv: 'Ich WERDE weitermachen' becomes 'Er sagte, er WERDE weitermachen.'

The Konjunktiv I operates in its own tense system, independent of the tense of the reporting verb.

In the indicative, you often shift into the Präteritum when reporting. The Konjunktiv I, by contrast, has its own three-tense system: present (sage), past (habe gesagt), future (werde sagen). This system is independent of whether the reporting verb is in the Präteritum or Perfekt. 'Heinrich sagte, die Backstube sei wunderbar' and 'Heinrich hat gesagt, die Backstube sei wunderbar' mean the same thing.
In indirect speech, past tense shifts to a special subjunctive form. The helping verb takes a new subjunctive ending while the past content is preserved.
Present tense in direct speech becomes Konjunktiv I Gegenwart in indirect speech.
Heinrich said he gets up early every morning.
Count the distinct grammatical moods in the three reported sentences. Two are reporting moods, one is a comparison mood. Are they all the same or different?
The first blank reports a past action in indirect speech; the second reports a timeless truth. Two different tenses of the same reporting mood.
Heinrich told that his grandfather had always said bread was the most important thing.
Learn the most important German proverbs. Practice all three grammar constructs in literary contexts. Understand proverbs as cultural values.
Learn the most important German proverbs
Practice all three grammar constructs in literary contexts
Understand proverbs as expressions of cultural values
The flashback deepens. 4 AM, 1962. Young Heinrich slides bread into the oven. His hands are covered in flour. The bakery glows warm against the dark outside. This is his world.
My father said the morning hour has gold in its mouth. He meant whoever gets up early owns the world. And he was right. The best ideas came to me before sunrise.
Heinrich reported that the best ideas had come to him before sunrise. He told it as if he were still in the bakery.
Papa. It is Sunday. You are in my living room. Drink your coffee.
The early bird catches the worm (lit: the morning hour has gold in its mouth)
Ancient German proverb praising early rising.\nArchaic form: 'Morgenstund' instead of 'Morgenstunde.'

A: Why do you get up so early?
B: The morning hour has gold in its mouth!
A: My grandfather always says that too.
B: Grandfathers are usually right.

Dating back centuries. 'Morgenstund' is an archaic form of 'Morgenstunde.' Gold in the mouth: the morning speaks richly. The German equivalent of 'The early bird catches the worm,' but far more poetic.
Practice makes perfect (lit: practice makes the master)
One of Germany's most used proverbs.\nNote: 'Meister' (master), not 'perfekt' (perfect).

A: I cannot do that well yet.
B: Practice makes the master!
A: How often do I have to practice?
B: Every day. The master does not fall from the sky.

The German version says 'Meister' (master), not 'perfect.' You can always keep learning. German humility: even mastery is a journey, not a destination. Paired with 'Es ist noch kein Meister vom Himmel gefallen.'
Every beginning is difficult
Universal comfort for starting something new.\nGermans cite it when things start badly.

A: I do not understand this grammar.
B: Every beginning is difficult.
A: That does not help me right now.
B: It does! It gets easier. Promise.
Universal comfort for anyone starting something new. Every language learner knows this truth. Encouragement disguised as resignation. The proverb validates the struggle.
Speech is silver, silence is gold
Praises knowing when NOT to speak.\nVery German: restraint valued over expression.

A: Should I say something about it?
B: Sometimes speech is silver and silence is gold.
A: You mean I should be quiet?
B: I mean not every thought needs words.
Praises the value of knowing when NOT to speak. Very German: restraint valued over expression. Used when someone talks too much or too impulsively. Silver is valuable, but gold is worth more.
Do not count your chickens before they hatch (lit: one should not praise the day before the evening)
German caution against premature celebration.\nWait for the outcome before judging.

A: I think I passed the exam!
B: One should not praise the day before the evening.
A: You mean I should wait?
B: Wait for the result. Then you can celebrate.
Do not celebrate too early. Wait for the outcome. German caution: success is only real when the day is done. Similar to 'Do not count your chickens before they hatch.'
Nobody is born an expert (lit: no master has yet fallen from the sky)
Mastery requires work. Nobody starts perfect.\nCompanion to 'Übung macht den Meister.'

A: I am so bad at this!
B: No master has yet fallen from the sky.
A: Do you think I can learn it?
B: Of course! Every master was once a beginner.
Nobody is born an expert. Mastery requires work. The companion to 'Übung macht den Meister.' Together they form a philosophy: practice patiently, nobody starts perfect.
Whoever laughs last laughs best
Patience wins. The long game matters.\nGerman love of delayed gratification.

A: They laughed at me.
B: Whoever laughs last laughs best.
A: That is true. I will show them.
B: Exactly. Patience pays off.
The early mockers may regret it. German love of the long game: the final outcome matters more than the initial reaction. Patience and persistence are deeply German values.
Different countries, different customs
Acknowledges cultural differences.\nNot judgmental but observational.

A: In my country we do that differently.
B: Different countries, different customs!
A: Exactly. Neither is better or worse.
B: Just different. And different is interesting.
The German acknowledgment that cultures differ. Used when something foreign seems strange. Not judgmental but observational. Verumius has lived this proverb every day since arriving in Germany.
If you start something, follow through (lit: whoever says A must also say B)
Commitment and follow-through.\nGerman consistency culture.

A: I started but it is hard.
B: Whoever says A must also say B.
A: You mean I have to continue?
B: You made your decision. Now follow through.
If you start something, see it through. German consistency culture: do not begin what you cannot finish. Once you commit, the alphabet demands you continue.
The apple does not fall far from the tree
Children resemble their parents.\nHeinrich's favorite proverb.

A: You bake just like your father!
B: The apple does not fall far from the tree.
A: That really is true for you.
B: I learned everything from him.
Heinrich's favorite: he became a baker like his grandfather. The Stamm (tree trunk) represents family roots. Nearly identical to the English version.
Still waters run deep
Quiet people may have hidden depth.\nGerman respect for introversion.

A: He never says much.
B: Still waters run deep.
A: You mean he thinks a lot?
B: The quiet ones often have the deepest thoughts.
A warning: do not underestimate the silent ones. Quiet people may have deep thoughts or hidden complexity. German respect for introversion. Nearly identical in English and German.
Where there is a will, there is a way
Determination creates solutions.\nThe same proverb exists in English.

A: I do not know how I am supposed to manage this.
B: Where there is a will, there is a way.
A: Do you really think so?
B: Always. The will finds the way.
Determination creates solutions. The same proverb exists in English. Universal human truth: willpower overcomes obstacles. One of the few German proverbs with an exact English equivalent.
the thumb
Masculine noun. Plural: die Daumen.\n'Jemandem die Daumen drücken' (to wish luck).

A: I have an exam tomorrow.
B: I am pressing my thumbs for you!
A: Thanks! Pressing thumbs always helps.
B: Well then, all thumbs are pressed!
'Jemandem die Daumen drücken' (to press thumbs for someone) is the German way of wishing luck. Not crossing fingers. Pressing thumbs. Physical gesture: fists with thumbs tucked inside.
the neck, the throat
Masculine noun. Plural: die Haelse.\n'Hals- und Beinbruch!' (Break a leg!).

A: I am so nervous before the performance.
B: Break your neck and leg!
A: That does not sound nice!
B: It does! It means 'Good luck!' in German.
'Hals- und Beinbruch!' (Neck and leg break!) means 'Break a leg!' German superstition: wishing bad luck to bring good luck. Ironic protection against fate.
the skin
Feminine noun. 'Mit Haut und Haar' means completely.\n'Er hat sich mit Haut und Haar verschrieben.'

A: He devoted himself completely to the bakery.
B: Yes, with skin and hair!
A: What does that mean exactly?
B: Entirely. Completely. With everything he has.
'Mit Haut und Haar' (with skin and hair) means completely, entirely. Body idioms are vivid in German. The Haut is the boundary between inside and outside, self and world.
the head
Masculine noun. Plural: die Koepfe.\n'Kopf hoch!' (Head up! / Chin up!).

A: I did not pass the exam.
B: Head up! Next time it will work.
A: Do you think so?
B: Yes. Do not stick your head in the sand.
'Den Kopf in den Sand stecken' (to stick your head in the sand) means to ignore problems. 'Kopf hoch!' (Head up!) is encouragement. The head hosts dozens of German Redewendungen.
the nail
Masculine noun. Plural: die Naegel.\n'Den Nagel auf den Kopf treffen' (to hit the nail on the head).

A: That was exactly right!
B: You hit the nail on the head.
A: Really?
B: Yes, right on target. Perfectly expressed.

Two idioms: 'Etwas auf den Nagel haengen' (to hang something on a nail) means to quit. 'Den Nagel auf den Kopf treffen' (to hit the nail on the head) means to be exactly right. Same noun, different wisdom.
the trunk, the stem
Masculine noun. Plural: die Staemme.\nFamily roots: 'Der Apfel fällt nicht weit vom Stamm.'

A: The trunk of the tree is very thick.
B: Yes, old trees have thick trunks.
A: And the family tree?
B: The apple does not fall far from the trunk.
'Stammtisch' (regular table at a pub), 'Stammkunde' (regular customer), 'Stammbaum' (family tree). The trunk from which branches grow. The Stamm is roots, tradition, belonging.
the butterfly
Masculine noun. Plural: die Schmetterlinge.\n'Schmetterlinge im Bauch' (butterflies in the stomach).

A: Do you have butterflies in your stomach?
B: Yes, I am so excited!
A: That is normal before the first meeting.
B: A thousand butterflies!

'Schmetterlinge im Bauch haben' (to have butterflies in your stomach) means to be in love or nervously excited. The German word comes from 'Schmetten' (cream), because butterflies were thought to steal cream.
to symbolize, to represent
'Ver-' + 'Sinnbild' (symbol) + '-lichen.' Not separable.\nThe most literary verb for expressing symbolic meaning.

A: What does bread symbolize in Heinrich's stories?
B: Bread symbolizes community and tradition.
A: And the bakery?
B: That symbolizes his life's work.
'Ver-' + 'Sinnbild' (symbol) + '-lichen.' Not separable. The most literary verb for expressing symbolic meaning. 'Brot versinnbildlicht Gemeinschaft' (Bread symbolizes community).
Wait until the day is over before judging it. German caution against premature celebration.
Indirect speech requires Konjunktiv I. Select the Konjunktiv I third-person singular form of the modal verb expressing necessity.
Heinrich said whoever says A must also say B.
The two sentences use different verb moods. One reports a fact from someone's mouth; the other describes a scenario that does not reflect current reality.
Two blanks, two tenses of reported speech. The first reports a past act; the second reports a timeless proverb truth. Both use the reporting mood.
Heinrich told that his grandfather had always said practice makes the master.
Test everything from Unit 28. Prove you can use indirect speech in Konjunktiv I, form unreal comparisons, and use German proverbs correctly.
Test your knowledge of all constructs from Unit 28
Prove Konjunktiv I, als ob, and proverb competence
Combine all grammar constructs in complex sentences
So. You listened. You wrote. Now I want to know whether you also understood. I will say a proverb, and you explain to me what it means. Figuratively and literally.
Now? Here? This is like an oral exam!
to press thumbs for someone (to wish luck)
The German way of wishing luck. Physical gesture:\nfists with thumbs tucked inside.

A: Tomorrow is my big exam.
B: I am pressing my thumbs for you!
A: Thanks, I need that.
B: Everyone is pressing their thumbs for you!
Not crossing fingers. Pressing thumbs. The German gesture of wishing luck. 'Ich drücke dir die Daumen!' is said before exams, job interviews, and first dates.
to give up, to quit (lit: to hang something on a nail)
Finality: once you hang your tools on the nail, you are done.\n'Er hat seinen Beruf an den Nagel gehaengt.'

A: Do you still bake?
B: No, I hung up baking.
A: Why?
B: It was time for something new.
Once you hang your tools on the nail, you are done. Finality. 'Er hat seinen Beruf an den Nagel gehaengt' (He gave up his profession). The nail is the endpoint.
to put your foot in it, to make a social blunder
The Fettnäpfchen was a grease pot by the door.\nStepping in it = social clumsiness.

A: I mispronounced his name.
B: Oh no, you stepped into the little grease pot!
A: That was so embarrassing.
B: That happens to everyone sometimes.
The Fettnäpfchen was a small container of grease kept by the door of old German houses. Stepping in it was clumsy and messy. The image became a metaphor for social blunders.
to never prosper (lit: to never get on a green branch)
Green branches are alive and growing.\nBeing stuck on a dead branch means stagnation.

A: He simply never prospers.
B: Yes, nothing works for him.
A: Maybe he needs help.
B: Or a new strategy.
Green branches are alive and growing. Being stuck on a dead branch means stagnation. Old German imagery from forestry. 'Auf keinen gruenen Zweig kommen' means to never succeed or prosper.
to not see the forest for the trees
Missing the big picture because of too many details.\nNearly identical in English and German.

A: I understand the individual words but not the sentence.
B: You cannot see the forest for the trees.
A: Exactly! Too many details.
B: Take a step back.
Perspective lost in complexity. The German version is nearly identical to the English one. Universal wisdom about the danger of losing the big picture in a sea of details.
to carry coals to Newcastle (lit: to carry owls to Athens)
Doing something pointless because it already exists in abundance.\nAthens's symbol was the owl.

A: Should I give him tips on baking?
B: That would be carrying owls to Athens!
A: True, he is a master baker.
B: Exactly. He does not need baking tips.
Athens's symbol was the owl (on coins, in art). Bringing more owls to Athens is pointless. Like bringing sand to the Sahara. The German idiom dates back to ancient references.
to flatter, to butter someone up (lit: to smear honey around someone's mouth)
Insincere praise. Sticky sweetness applied to win favor.\n'Er schmiert ihr Honig ums Maul.'

A: You are the best cook in the world!
B: Do not smear honey around my mouth.
A: I mean it!
B: Still. Honesty is better than honey.
Sticky sweetness applied to make someone feel good. Insincere praise. 'Er schmiert ihr Honig ums Maul' = He is flattering her. The honey-mouth image is vivid and unforgettable.
to have butterflies in your stomach
Being nervously in love or excited.\nSame image as in English.

A: Do you have butterflies in your stomach?
B: Yes, a thousand of them!
A: That is a good sign.
B: Or a sign that I am nervous.

The fluttering feeling of nervous excitement. Same image as English 'butterflies in your stomach.' Verumius knows this feeling from his first day in Germany. Love and anxiety share the same butterflies.
to throw the baby out with the bathwater
Discarding something valuable along with what is unwanted.\nMedieval origin: shared bathwater.

A: I want to change everything!
B: Be careful not to throw the baby out with the bathwater.
A: You mean I should keep the good parts?
B: Exactly. Do not throw everything away.
Medieval origin: the whole family shared one bath. The baby bathed last in dirty water. Careful what you throw away! A warning against destroying the good while removing the bad.
to sneak away (lit: to make oneself out of the dust)
Disappearing quietly, often to avoid responsibility.\n'Er hat sich aus dem Staub gemacht.'

A: Where did he go?
B: He sneaked away.
A: Just like that? Without a word?
B: Yes, vanished like dust in the wind.
Disappearing like dust in the wind. Gone without a trace. Often used for people avoiding responsibility. 'Er hat sich aus dem Staub gemacht' implies a deliberate, quiet departure.
to be on the wrong track (lit: to be on the wood path)
A Holzweg leads only to a logging site, not a destination.\n'Da bist du auf dem Holzweg!'

A: I think the word means 'fish.'
B: You are on the wrong track!
A: Really? What does it mean then?
B: Something completely different. Try again.
A Holzweg is a path that leads only to a logging site, not a destination. Following it means you end up nowhere. 'Da bist du auf dem Holzweg!' = You are mistaken! Forest paths that deceive.
to be oblivious (lit: to have tomatoes on your eyes)
Not seeing what is right in front of you.\n'Hast du Tomaten auf den Augen?'

A: I cannot see the sign.
B: Do you have tomatoes on your eyes?
A: Where is it then?
B: Right in front of you! Big red letters!
Red tomatoes blocking your vision. Used when someone misses the obvious. 'Hast du Tomaten auf den Augen?' = Are you blind? A uniquely German image for obliviousness.
the little grease pot
Neuter noun. 'Fett' (fat/grease) + 'Naepfchen' (little pot).\nSource of the idiom 'ins Fettnäpfchen treten.'

A: What is a Fettnäpfchen?
B: A small pot with grease, formerly by the door.
A: And when you step in it?
B: Then you have made a mistake!
A small container of grease kept by the door of old German houses. The diminutive '-naepfchen' makes it neuter. Stepping in it was a social metaphor for clumsiness that survives in modern German.
Think of someone vanishing without a sound, like dust dispersed by the wind, often to escape responsibility.
Understand and explain German idioms. Describe their meanings, origins, and how they reflect German culture and values.
Learn verbs for describing and explaining
Understand how idioms are transmitted
Use metaphorical language in context
to describe
'Be-' + 'schreiben' (to write). Not separable.\nStrong verb: beschreiben/beschrieb/beschrieben.

A: Can you describe the bakery?
B: Heinrich described it as warm and full of flour.
A: How did he describe it exactly?
B: With many figurative expressions.
'Be-' + 'schreiben' (to write). Covering something with writing (words). Not separable. Strong verb: beschreiben/beschrieb/beschrieben. 'Beschreibung' = description.
to interpret, to lay out
'Aus' (out) + 'legen' (to lay). Separable.\n'Wie legen Sie dieses Sprichwort aus?'

A: How do you interpret this proverb?
B: I interpret it this way: patience is important.
A: Can you interpret it differently too?
B: Yes, everyone interprets proverbs differently.
Separable: 'Wie legen Sie dieses Sprichwort aus?' Interpretation: laying out the possible meanings. 'Auslegung' = interpretation. Also: to display goods in a shop.
to clarify, to make clear
'Ver-' + 'deutlich' (clear) + '-en.' Not separable.\nStronger than 'erklären.'

A: Can you clarify that?
B: Yes, I will clarify it with an example.
A: That clarifies the matter.
B: Good. Clarity is the goal.
Making something 'deutlich' (clear). Stronger than 'erklären' (to explain). Not separable. 'Können Sie das verdeutlichen?' is a polite request for more clarity. 'Verdeutlichung' = clarification.
to reflect, to mirror
'Wider' (against/back) + 'spiegeln' (to mirror).\n'Sprichwörter spiegeln die Kultur wider.'

A: What do proverbs reflect?
B: They reflect the values of a culture.
A: And Heinrich's stories?
B: They reflect his entire life.
Culture reflected back like in a mirror. 'Wider' (against/back) + 'spiegeln' (to mirror). Separable: 'Sprichwörter spiegeln die Kultur wider.' 'Widerspiegelung' = reflection.
to hand down, to transmit through generations
'Über' (over) + 'liefern' (to deliver). Not separable.\n'Mündlich überlieferte Weisheiten.'

A: How were proverbs handed down?
B: Orally, from generation to generation.
A: Nobody wrote them down?
B: Only later. Before that they were just passed down.
Delivering over time. 'Über' (over) + 'liefern' (to deliver). Not separable. 'Überlieferung' = tradition, transmission. How wisdom travels across centuries.
to spread, to disseminate
'Ver-' + 'breit' (broad/wide) + '-en.' Not separable.\n'Sprichwörter verbreiten Weisheit.'

A: How do proverbs spread?
B: Through telling. People spread them.
A: Also through books?
B: Yes, but oral dissemination came first.
Making something wider. 'Ver-' + 'breit' (broad/wide). Not separable. 'Verbreitung' = dissemination. How wisdom travels from person to person, getting broader with each telling.
to take up, to pick up on
'Auf' (up) + 'greifen' (to grab). Separable.\n'Er griff das Thema auf.'

A: Verumius picks up Heinrich's proverbs.
B: Yes, he picks them up and writes them down.
A: That way they are preserved.
B: Exactly. To pick up means to preserve.
Picking up a motif, a proverb, or an idea from someone else's words. Separable: 'Er griff das Thema auf.' Strong verb: aufgreifen/griff auf/aufgegriffen.
to state, to express
'Aus' (out) + 'sagen' (to say). Separable.\n'Was sagt dieses Sprichwort aus?'

A: What does this proverb express?
B: It expresses that patience is important.
A: And what does Heinrich's story express?
B: It expresses that tradition counts.
The meaning that comes OUT of the words. 'Aus' (out) + 'sagen' (to say). Separable: 'Was sagt dieses Sprichwort aus?' 'Aussage' = statement, meaning.
A Holzweg is a forest path that ends in a dead end. Following it means going nowhere.
to laugh
Regular verb. Ich lache, du lachst, er lacht.\n'Wer zuletzt lacht, lacht am besten.'

A: What are you laughing about?
B: About Heinrich's story. He laughs most of all himself.
A: Laughing is healthy.
B: Yes, and proverbs that make you laugh stay with you.

One of the most human actions there is. 'Das Lachen' (the laughter) is the nominalized form. Sprichwort: 'Wer zuletzt lacht, lacht am besten' (He who laughs last, laughs best). The Konjunktiv I form 'lache' appears in indirect speech reporting.
The reported predicate needs Konjunktiv I, even when the wer-clause uses Indikativ. Apply the reporting mood to the main verb.
Heinrich said whoever laughs last laughs best.
als ob + hätte (Konj. II) describes an unreal scenario: he acts AS IF he had tomatoes in front of his eyes.
to carry, to wear, to bear
Strong verb. Ich trage, du trägst, er trägt.\nIrregular: a > ae in du/er forms.

A: What does Heinrich carry on his arm?
B: An old bakery in memory.
A: How does one carry on a tradition?
B: By telling it.

Strong verb with vowel change: tragen/trug/getragen. 'Eulen nach Athen tragen' (to carry owls to Athens) is a classic Redewendung meaning to do something pointless. The Konjunktiv I form 'trage' marks indirect speech.
Indirect speech requires the reporting mood. Select the form of 'tragen' that signals reported observation, not a direct factual statement.
Hildi remarked that her father sometimes carries owls to Athens.
Three sentences: the first reports a past action, the second makes an unreal comparison, the third reports a current state. Identify the mood in each.
Not bad, Verumius. You listened. You understood. My grandfather would have said: practice makes the master. And you practice well.
Come back next Sunday. Papa has a whole book of proverbs in his head. And I have cake.
The final proverbs and idioms. Review all constructs from Unit 28. Celebrate the meeting of oral tradition and foreign learner.
Master the remaining proverbs and idioms
Review all constructs from Unit 28
Celebrate the meeting of oral tradition and language learner

Evening. Verumius's apartment. Desk lamp on. He transcribes Heinrich's proverbs into a notebook. Each one gets a page: the proverb, the story, the meaning. A growing collection of oral tradition preserved.
Every proverb has a story. Figuratively speaking, they are like time capsules. Heinrich entrusted them to me as if I belonged to the family.

He writes the last one: 'As one calls into the forest, so it echoes back.' Closes the notebook. Smiles. Somewhere across Berlin, Opa Heinrich is probably telling the same stories to someone else, each time a little different.

Next week: a public debate. Verumius's German is now good enough for the arena of ideas. But first, he texts Hildi: 'Thanks for the Sunday. And thanks to Opa Heinrich.' She replies: 'He says you should come back next Sunday. He has more.'
to kill two birds with one stone (lit: to hit two flies with one swatter)
Accomplishing two things at once.\nThe German version uses a fly swatter, not a stone.

A: I am going to the supermarket and picking up the mail.
B: Two flies with one swatter!
A: Exactly, why go twice?
B: Efficient. Very German.

Same idea as English 'kill two birds with one stone,' but the German version is less violent: a fly swatter (Klappe) instead of a stone. Accomplishing two things at once. German efficiency in proverb form.
to buy a pig in a poke (lit: to buy the cat in the bag)
Buying something sight unseen.\nMedieval origin: dishonest merchants sold cats instead of rabbits.

A: I am buying the car without a test drive.
B: Do not buy the cat in the bag!
A: You mean I should test it first?
B: Of course! Open the bag first.
Medieval origin: dishonest merchants sold cats instead of rabbits in closed bags. 'Pig in a poke' is the English equivalent. Trust but verify. Always open the bag before paying.
What you miss in childhood is hard to learn later
Haenschen = little Hans (child). Hans = grown man.\nArgument for early education.

A: I should have learned German earlier.
B: What little Hans does not learn, big Hans never learns!
A: But I am learning it now!
B: True. You are the exception!
Haenschen = little Hans (child). Hans = grown man. A German argument for early education. What the child misses, the adult cannot recover. Though Verumius proves that adults can learn too.
You get what you give (lit: as one calls into the forest, so it echoes back)
Treat people well and they treat you well.\nThe forest as mirror of behavior.

A: Why is he so unfriendly to me?
B: As you call into the forest, so it echoes back.
A: You mean because I was unfriendly first?
B: Exactly. The forest gives back what it receives.
