The forest as mirror of behavior. Action and reaction. Treat people well and they treat you well. The last proverb Verumius writes in his notebook. A fitting end: language learning echoes back what you put in.
figuratively speaking
Fixed phrase. 'Bildlich' (figuratively) + 'gesprochen' (spoken).\nAnnounces that what follows is not literal.

A: Language is a bridge.
B: Figuratively speaking, yes.
A: Of course figuratively speaking!
B: Sometimes the best truths are figurative.
The polite preface to a metaphor. 'Bildlich gesprochen ist die Sprache eine Brücke.' Signals to the listener: what follows is not literal but carries a deeper meaning through imagery.
according to reports, reportedly
Very formal. Newspaper register.\nAttributing without committing to the truth.

A: According to reports, the company is planning changes.
B: Who says that?
A: I heard it. According to reports.
B: So just rumors. Not yet confirmed.
Very formal. 'Dem Vernehmen' (the hearing/perception) + 'nach' (according to). Newspaper register. Attributing a claim without committing to its truth. Distance through formality.
so to speak
'So' + 'zu' + 'sagen.' A softener for approximate language.\nConversational and versatile.

A: He is, so to speak, our proverb expert.
B: So to speak? So not officially?
A: No, but he knows the most about it.
B: Then he is, so to speak, the best.
A softener that signals approximate or figurative language. 'Er ist sozusagen unser Experte' hedges the claim gently. Conversational and versatile. Three words joined into one adverb.
supposed, alleged
'Ver-' + 'meintlich' (from 'meinen'). Casts doubt.\n'Der vermeintliche Experte' (the supposed expert).

A: The supposed expert was wrong.
B: Supposed? So not a real expert?
A: Exactly. Only supposedly.
B: Supposed knowledge can be dangerous.

Casts doubt: someone is thought to be something, but maybe is not. 'Der vermeintliche Experte' (the supposed expert). Polite skepticism. From 'meinen' (to think/suppose) with the doubting prefix 'ver-.'
taken literally
'Wörtlich' (literally) + 'genommen' (taken).\nThe opposite of 'bildlich gesprochen.'

A: Taken literally, 'Morgenstund' means the early hour.
B: But figuratively speaking it means more.
A: Exactly. Literal is only the surface.
B: The depth lies in the figurative sense.
The opposite of 'bildlich gesprochen.' Surface meaning only. 'Wörtlich genommen bedeutet Morgenstund die frühe Stunde.' Understanding words at face value, without metaphorical interpretation.
as if (one) had
'Als' + 'hätte' (Konjunktiv II of haben).\nShort form, more elegant than 'als ob er hätte.'

A: He tells it as if he had experienced it yesterday.
B: Yes, as if he had stopped time.
A: The memory is very vivid.
B: As if it had never ended.
The short 'als + Konjunktiv II' form. More elegant than 'als ob er gehabt hätte.' Literary register. 'Er erzählt es, als hätte er es gestern erlebt' compresses unreal comparison into three words.
to refer to
Reflexive. Not separable. 'Das Sprichwort bezieht sich auf...'\nEssential for literary analysis.

A: What does this proverb refer to?
B: It refers to patience and perseverance.
A: And Heinrich's story?
B: That refers to his youth.
Reflexive. Not separable. 'Das Sprichwort bezieht sich auf...' (The proverb refers to...). Essential for literary analysis. The 'auf' directs the reference to its target.
to paraphrase, to describe in other words
Not separable (stress on 'schreiben'). 'Um' (around) + 'schreiben' (to write).\n'Können Sie das umschreiben?'

A: Can you paraphrase the proverb?
B: Yes: whoever is patient wins in the end.
A: That is a good paraphrase.
B: Sometimes you have to paraphrase proverbs.

Saying the same thing in different words. 'Um' (around) + 'schreiben' (to write). Not separable when it means 'to paraphrase' (stress on schreiben). When separable (stress on um), it means 'to rewrite.'
to imply, to insinuate
Not separable. 'Unter' (under) + 'stellen' (to place).\nPlacing a meaning underneath someone's words.

A: What are you implying about me?
B: Nothing! I am not implying anything.
A: But it sounded that way.
B: I am sorry. That was not my intention.
Placing a meaning underneath someone's words. 'Was wollen Sie mir unterstellen?' (What are you implying about me?). Can be accusatory. Not separable. The hidden meaning placed 'under' the surface.
to justify, to give reasons for
'Be-' + 'gründen' (to found). Not separable.\nLaying the foundation (Grund) for an argument.

A: Can you justify your opinion?
B: Yes, I justify it with Heinrich's experience.
A: That is well justified.
B: An opinion without justification is weak.
'Be-' + 'gründen' (to found). Laying the foundation (Grund = reason/ground) for an argument. Not separable. 'Begründung' = justification. In German academic culture, everything must be begründet.
to reply, to retort
'Ent-' + 'gegnen' (from 'gegen,' against). Not separable.\nMore confrontational than 'antworten.'

A: That is wrong.
B: Hildi retorted that it had been half past three.
A: What did Heinrich reply to that?
B: He retorted that it was a detail.
A response that pushes back. More confrontational than 'antworten.' 'Ent-' + 'gegnen' (from 'gegen,' against). Formal debate register. Hildi's corrections are gentle Entgegnungen.
to convey, to mediate
'Ver-' + 'Mittel' (means/middle). Not separable.\n'Sprichwörter vermitteln Weisheit.'

A: What do proverbs convey?
B: They convey wisdom and values.
A: And Heinrich conveys them to Verumius.
B: Exactly. He is the mediator between generations.
Placing something in the middle between sender and receiver. 'Ver-' + 'Mittel' (means/middle). Not separable. 'Vermittlung' = mediation, conveyance. Sprichwörter vermitteln Weisheit across time.
to be called, to mean
Strong verb: heissen/hiess/geheissen.\n'Was heißt das?' (What does that mean?). Abbreviation: d.h.

A: What does this proverb mean?
B: It means that patience pays off.
A: That means I should wait?
B: That means everything needs its time.
The most basic word for 'to mean.' 'Was heißt das?' (What does that mean?). Strong verb: heissen/hiess/geheissen. 'Das heißt...' abbreviated as 'd.h.' is everywhere in German writing.

Verumius, writing down Heinrich's proverbs, is capturing something real. Germany has a rich oral tradition: Maerchen (Grimm), proverbs, idioms. They were passed down orally for centuries before anyone wrote them down. As a language learner, you are now part of this chain. When you learn a proverb, you carry it forward. Heinrich's stories exist because someone listened and someone wrote. Learning a language is cultural preservation.

The Brothers Grimm collected fairy tales from storytellers and wrote them down. Before that, these stories existed only in people's voices. The same is true of proverbs: 'Morgenstund hat Gold im Mund' was said hundreds of times before anyone committed it to writing. Today, learners like Verumius preserve this tradition by listening, understanding, and passing it on.
Haenschen (little Hans) = childhood. Hans = adulthood. What the child misses, the adult cannot make up for.
Konjunktiv I of 'sein' in indirect speech. Plural form because 'Sprichwörter' is plural.
Heinrich told that the old proverbs were timeless.
Count the reporting mood forms in Hildi's three statements, then identify the one verb form that instead expresses an unreal scenario.
to call into (something)
Hinein (into) + rufen (to call). Separable verb.\n'In den Wald hineinrufen' = to call into the forest.

A: Why are you calling into the forest?
B: I am testing whether the echo answers.
A: Whoever calls into the forest...
B: ...gets it back. The proverb is true.

Hinein (into) + rufen (to call). Separable: 'Er ruft in den Wald hinein.' 'Wie man in den Wald hineinruft, so schallt es heraus' is one of Germany's most famous Sprichwörter: you get back what you put out. The Konjunktiv I form 'hineinrufe' signals indirect speech.
Both blanks report the proverb in indirect speech. Apply the reporting mood to both verbs: the wer-clause verb and the main predicate.
Heinrich said whoever calls into the forest gets it back the same way.
